title: Systems Biology — The Top-Down Sibling Discipline

lines.text: Section six. Systems biology — synthetic biology's top-down sibling.
There is a related discipline that emerged around the same time as synthetic biology, building on the same enabling technologies, called systems biology.
The two are often confused, and it is worth being clear about how they differ.
Systems biology started, roughly speaking, after the first genome sequences were completed.
The motivating observation was that biology had moved from a regime of data scarcity to a regime of data abundance.
Genome sequencing produced millions of base pairs of DNA data per project.
DNA microarrays, the canonical example, measure the expression level of every gene in a cell simultaneously, producing snapshots of which genes are on, which are off, and by how much.
Proteomics measures all the proteins in a cell.
Metabolomics measures all the metabolites.
These high-throughput techniques produce data at a scale that no single researcher can simply look at and intuit a story from.
Systems biology's answer is mathematical and computational.

Build in silico mathematical models of how the cell operates, fit those models against the high-throughput experimental data, and use the model to infer how the cell actually works — which interactions, feedback loops, and regulatory motifs explain the observed behaviour.

Where traditional reductionist biology takes a single protein or pathway and characterises it in detail, systems biology takes the whole cell as the unit of analysis and tries to derive its structure from holistic measurements.
Synthetic biology shares much of the modelling machinery with systems biology — ordinary differential equations, stochastic simulations, Boolean networks, the same mathematical vocabulary.
But the direction of inference is reversed.
Systems biology is top-down: measure cellular outcomes, build a model that explains them.
Synthetic biology is bottom-up: characterise parts, combine them deliberately to produce desired outcomes, then use models to predict and verify.
The textbook puts it cleanly: systems biology infers function from data; synthetic biology builds function into cells.
These two disciplines are, in many ways, intellectual siblings, and the dialogue between them has been productive in both directions.
Systems biologists have provided synthetic biologists with quantitative characterisations of natural regulatory networks that can be co-opted into engineered designs.

Synthetic biologists have provided systems biologists with controlled perturbations of cells — engineered circuits whose behaviour is by definition known, which let systems biologists test their models against ground truth.
If you read papers in either field, you will often see authors who work across both, and the methods sections look strikingly similar.

The distinction matters mostly in framing: do you start from data and seek understanding, or do you start from a design goal and seek a working implementation? Synthetic biology is the second of those.
And the field as a whole has benefited enormously from the maturation of systems biology, which had a head start on the mathematics by perhaps a decade.
